text:
Audio: Good morning, everyone. Welcome to our ordinary list discourse lecture. Most of those songs had ordinary and so kind of picking the right song for today. So today is going to be the last kind of linear regression lecture that we have. And starting from next lecture, we're going to move on to a more application side of linear regression that you're seeing or you have seen so far. So kind of a teaser of today's lecture, I gave it to you in the previous lecture I talked about the fact that today we're going to do multiple linear regression And what we're going to do is that we're going to have multiple different Attributes or features that we want to combine in order to make a specific prediction So this is kind of the outline for today. I'm going to talk about Walk you through the modeling process the same forest that process that we saw previously with the simple linear regression and also with the constant model But this time we're going to see that with a multiple linear regression And then at the end we're going to talk about some of the properties of OLS or generally ceSquares and we're going to talk about evaluating the model that we have built so I'm going to start by introducing what what multiple linear regression is. But before diving into the content of today's lecture, I also wanna give you a warning that every section of today's lecture is just gonna be me reviewing or reminding you of some linear algebra concepts and then using them. text:
Audio: So it's just one dimensional data point of skill or value as the output. So I also gave you an example last time, a very simple example, or instead of having a constant model or having one feature using some and use a simple linear regression, what if I have two features? What if I have different snapshots of the lengths and weight of my cat over the years, and I want to use this data so that in future, future if I see a cat I can predict their age using the length and the weight. What I'll do is I'll go ahead and collect a bunch of different data points for name is Pishy. So we can see that likes just trying to stretch herself to get different lengths and then I'll collect another data point and so on and so forth. And what I'll do is I'll plug in all of these attributes that I have into a multiple regression model assuming that I have the parameters known for the weight and the length. So this is a parameter for the lengths, for example. This is a parameter for the weight. If I plug in any feature, any entry, or any observation for a cat such as, again, the lengths and the weight, what I can do is I can calculate a weighted combination of all of these features that I have. And then add on also the tetasero value and then be able to get why I had or the projection for the age. So this is kind of the simplest case that you have two features. But we're going to move on to using more features and try to scale this beyond two features and kind of generalize what kind of space we're living in with multiple regression. text:
Audio: So today's lecture is gonna use a data set of NBA players from 2018, 2019, and we have different attributes from different players that we can use. The target or the target variable that we want to measure or we want to predict is the average number of points that those players are going to score in a given game. So that's the target value. That's this entire column that we have, but then we also have some input feature values that we can use. The input feature values that we have are going to be field goal. So the field goal is the number of two pointers that these players would score on average in a game. We also have a feature ASC or the number of average number of assists that they have in a given game. So this is the assists column. And then there is also a TPA or the number of tree pointers that they would score. And this is also the number of, this is exactly that feature. So you can imagine that. In a regular scenario that we have a data frame with multiple different columns, this is how we would, this is kind of the data set or the shape of the data that we want to deal with, and we want to use this information in order to be able to predict that having information about field goal assist 3-PA4, another different player can be learned parameters so that we can calculate a weighted combination of those features together in order to predict the average number of points that they would score in a given game. text:
Audio: Okay, so this is going to be where the start of the magic is going to be happening. So why do I need to go through this entire, like, making the equation, uh, an equation that involves matrices? It's because the nature of my data right now is going to be different. Uh, for the simple linear regression, remember that I had one feature vector, and then I also had one target vector, uh, or one feature input column, and then one target vector column, um, that I had. And then I could directly use all of these values that I have for the features in order to get all of those predictions. and it was simply a bunch of different scalar values that I needed to deal with. So it was easy to calculate them and sum them and so on and so forth. When I was doing a constant model, there was no input feature. It was just the target values that I had for all of those YIs. And depending on the last function, we saw that there is going to be different ways of coming up with what the optimum tetasera hat value is going to be for the constant model. In this current case that we're dealing with, we have multiple different observations. So we will have those rows. Remember that here we have also row one, row two all the way to row n. We have n different data points. Here is also the same case that we have n different data points, but it just happens that you're only keeping track of the target values that we have. In the multiple regression case, what I have is still the same scenario where I have one observation as the entire row, but the issue is that my entire row is now involved with multiple different columns that I have. audio: Okay, so this is going to be where the start of the magic is going to be happening. So why do I need to go through this entire, like, making the equation, uh, an equation that involves matrices? It's because the nature of my data right now is going to be different. Uh, for the simple linear regression, remember that I had one feature vector, and then I also had one target vector, uh, or one feature input column, and then one target vector column, um, that I had. And then I could directly use all of these values that I have for the features in order to get all of those predictions. and it was simply a bunch of different scalar values that I needed to deal with. So it was easy to calculate them and sum them and so on and so forth. When I was doing a constant model, there was no input feature. It was just the target values that I had for all of those YIs. And depending on the last function, we saw that there is going to be different ways of coming up with what the optimum tetasera hat value is going to be for the constant model. In this current case that we're dealing with, we have multiple different observations. So we will have those rows. Remember that here we have also row one, row two all the way to row n. We have n different data points. Here is also the same case that we have n different data points, but it just happens that you're only keeping track of the target values that we have. In the multiple regression case, what I have is still the same scenario where I have one observation as the entire row, but the issue is that my entire row is now involved with multiple different columns that I have.

Okay, let's see how that's going to be happening. If I'm going to think about how I will look at this matrix and what each one of those rows represent and what each one of those columns represent and what are those. complicated indices that you see on the matrix. The way that you can think about it is that each one of the rows is one observation one row in your data frame. Right. And one row in the data frame again has multiple different features. And the way that I'm going to look at each one of these features is that, for example, I would say for observation, I. The first index of all of these attributes is always going to be I representing that this is ICE observation. But then as I'm going through multiple different features here, I would say that this is feature one of observation, i, this is feature two of observation, i, and then all the way to feature P of observation, i. So for example, if you're looking at one entire column, what you will notice is that all of these, all of the second indices or second subscripts are exactly the same. So for example, I'm looking at the second column, second feature. So all of the second values are two. But what you're noticing here is that the first index value is one, two, all the way to end, showing that this is feature two of observation, one feature two of observation, two all the way to feature two of observation in. So that's how I want you to think about it. And if you're looking at how this matrix is going to be represented in your data frame, it's exactly as that. And every time that you had a data frame, you had rows representing observations and columns representing different attributes or different features in the data frame. And that's how we're going to treat that in the matrix word as well. So if I'm going to go ahead and write the multiple linear regression, remember that multiple linear regression was telling me that? a wall that the prediction is going to be the weighted combination of those features weighted by the parameters that I have. So for example here I would say that this is feature one that is multiplied by the weight for feature one and this is also I would say feature two that is multiplied by the weight for feature two and then all the way to feature p which was green that is multiplied by the parameter for feature P, right? So effectively, what I can do is I can apply this equation to all of the rows. So I can go ahead and get the prediction for all of the observations that I have. Because this is a general form observation that I'll, that I'm showing in this case. text:
Audio: And what dot product means? You probably have this from your Mass 54, or any other linear algebra course that you've taken. But the dot product between two vectors means that we're going to take a look at the two vectors should have the same size as the starter. And then you're going to multiply corresponding elements with each other. And then you're going to take the summation of all of those products. And then the final number is going to be a scalar value number or a real value number that you can use as the dot product between two vectors. vectors. So for example in this case if I have the two vectors u and v Each one of them is three-dimensional. What I will do is in order to calculate the dot product between these two vectors, I need to multiply corresponding elements and then I'll sum up all of those products, and then it will give me a single value. So if I'm thinking about this as how to calculate exactly the dot product between the vector v and vector u, I can also think about transposing one of them and multiplying it with the other vector. So again, in this case, this would be equal to saying that, okay, I'm going to transpose, for example, the vector u, this is going to give me 1, 2, 3, and then I'm going to multiply it with the column vector 1, 1, 1, 1. This is very simple, 1 row, 1 row vector, 1 column vector, so you would just do the normal matrix multiplication, which is going to give you 1 times 1 plus 2 times 1 plus 3 times 1, which is equal to 6. audio: And what dot product means? You probably have this from your Mass 54, or any other linear algebra course that you've taken. But the dot product between two vectors means that we're going to take a look at the two vectors should have the same size as the starter. And then you're going to multiply corresponding elements with each other. And then you're going to take the summation of all of those products. And then the final number is going to be a scalar value number or a real value number that you can use as the dot product between two vectors. vectors. So for example in this case if I have the two vectors u and v Each one of them is three-dimensional. What I will do is in order to calculate the dot product between these two vectors, I need to multiply corresponding elements and then I'll sum up all of those products, and then it will give me a single value. So if I'm thinking about this as how to calculate exactly the dot product between the vector v and vector u, I can also think about transposing one of them and multiplying it with the other vector. So again, in this case, this would be equal to saying that, okay, I'm going to transpose, for example, the vector u, this is going to give me 1, 2, 3, and then I'm going to multiply it with the column vector 1, 1, 1, 1. This is very simple, 1 row, 1 row vector, 1 column vector, so you would just do the normal matrix multiplication, which is going to give you 1 times 1 plus 2 times 1 plus 3 times 1, which is equal to 6.

text:
Audio: equal to 10 to 0 plus the dot for like between those two vectors that I have. So it's going to be much simpler. Imagine that you will have data sets with millions of different features and you don't want to calculate those products manually and then some of all of those products manually in order to calculate in order to just do a simple prediction. Let alone doing optimization later on that you will see that it's going to be more complicated. So here I'm calculating the product between two vectors. This can be optimized and parallelized, and then I'm just summing up tetasero as a second term. But still, I'm going to be a little bit greedy and I would say that I want everything to just be written as the duck product between two vectors. I don't want to have one duck product plus the summation. Is there a way that I can move pet to zero also into the duck product calculation that I have. The issue that I wasn't able to do it initially was that each one of these tetan- tetan- i values they had kind of a partner. This one had a partner of x1, this one had a partner of x2, this one had a partner of xp, so that's why I was able to just collect all the partners into one column vector x and then all of the tetan- values into tetan vector. But then what I didn't have for Tetra 0 was that kind of partner that multiplied 2 tetra 0. But I can use a trick. What I can do is I can say that what if I assume that theta zero was multiplied by one? Video: 1: Lec 12 - DS100 Fa24 - Ordinary

Audio: What I want you to notice is that this heta vector is going to be a vector that's in common between all of those rows. So why am I going to separate them into multiple different equations? Uh. multiple equations in the case that I could also aggregate all of those rows together and then multiply all of those rows together with the single petal vector that they have in common Again, if you take a look at what's happening with the NBA data set, so this is exactly the single observation that I have with three different features, and this is the target value that I have. And in order for me to make a prediction, what I can do is I can say that my observation is now going to be 0.4, 0.4 right here. 0.8, 0.8 right here as a feature and then 1.5, 1.5 right here. And then notice here that I have this one, which is the augmented bias that I added. And this is in fact called a bias trick because the trick that I use in order to find that to zero kind of company or something as the value one that then I Combined it with all the all the rest of the X's or the features that then I can have everything written as the doc product Within two vectors. This is called a bias trick And this entry is actually going to be related to a feature that's simulating what's going to be multiplied by the tetah 0. Video: 1: Lec 12 - DS100 Fa24 - Ordinary

One thing that I want you to notice is the dimensionality of all of these vectors that I'm dealing with. Because this dimensionality is going to be very helpful moving on. So the dimensionality of x2 or the observation, I'm saying that it's in r4 or is in the 4 dimensional space. It is correct because I have three different features, field goal assist and TPA, and then I added one more dimension for this bias term that I added right here. So always if you have p different features, the dimensionality of your observations is going to be p plus 1. He attributes for p different attributes that you obviously have for any observation and then one for the bias trick or the bias term that you're adding on top of that. The tetah vector is always going to be p plus 1 dimensional. So for example, in this case, it's going to be equal to 4 because I have 3 features plus the bias that to 0 that makes 4 different parameters. So it's always going to be p plus 1 again if you have p different features. And always the target value and then the prediction value is going to be a real value number. So the dimensionality of x2 or the observation, I'm saying that it's in r4 or is in the 4 dimensional space. It is correct because I have three different features, field goal assist and TPA, and then I added one more dimension for this bias term that I added right here. So always if you have p different features, the dimensionality of your observations is going to be p plus 1. He attributes for p different attributes that you obviously have for any observation and then one for the bias trick or the bias term that you're adding on top of that. The tetah vector is always going to be p plus 1 dimensional. So for example, in this case, it's going to be equal to 4 because I have 3 features plus the bias that to 0 that makes 4 different parameters. So it's always going to be p plus 1 again if you have p different features. And always the target value and then the prediction value is going to be a real value number.

Audio: So it's just the scalar value that we're predicting as the output. for not predicting a vector or predicting a single value. Because that's what the dot product is going to give you at the end. Okay. So let's take a look at what's happening here. These are all the equations that I had. I was saying that in order to get the prediction for data point one, I can go ahead and transpose the augmented observation that noticed that is augmented with this bias trick or this additional dimension that I added, multiplied with the tetavector that I have. I can write exactly the same thing for all of the different data points that I have, such as Y2 and then Y2 hat and then YN hat and get all the predictions for all of the different vectors. are different rows that I have. One thing that I told you before is that if you notice that this TETA vector is the same or is as common between all of those rows and instead of me calculating all of those values independently. What if I combine all of these x i transpose values into a matrix and just go ahead and multiply that matrix by the same TETA vector that all of those rows are going to be multiplied to. So in that case, if I'm treating this as an entire matrix, the matrix multiplication by default is going to go and multiply the first row with the tetavector and then it's going to go ahead and multiply the second row with the tetavector and so on and so forth. So all of those predictions are going to be in one place. this was exactly your data frame right you just augmented it with one column of all of these one values. Once you have the TETA vector, you can just plug in the TETA vector right here. And you would multiply your entire... your entire data frame with that set of vector and as a result you're going to get all of those predictions in one shot in one vector. Right, so very, very kind of pretty way of doing all the predictions all advanced using a simple matrix multiplication. This matrix that I created noticed that it has n rows and it has p plus one different columns. And for each one of the observations obviously because I'm stacking them together and then p plus one for the number of columns because there is one column for all of those values of ones and then p for p different features that we are using in order to make the prediction. We talked a lot about, we have this multiple regression model that we call it a linear model. We had seen that a multiple regression model... or a linear model is a model that is a weighted combination of tetas, meaning that we can factor out the tetah vector as one vector and then multiply all the weights and all of the parameters or features as a separate matrix to it. And in that case we would call a model, a linear model in tetah. So the question for you is that which one of these five options are linear in TETTA based on the description that I gave you. Audio: So they have a matrix that is multiplied by a vector of all of those theta values that I have. But we can go and take a look at one, two, and three, and then see which one of them are linear in tetan, which one of them are not linear in tetan. So it's starting with the first one. So the first one is linear in tetan because I can write it as the dot product between the two vectors and the two vectors are one vector fully have all the tetan values and non-transform tetan values. values and then another vector has all of those other values that I don't care if they're linear, they're not linear and whatever value they have. The only thing that I care about is that I can write my equation in a way that all the tetas values can be factored out into a vector. So here they're factored out into a vector, here they're factored out into a vector and option one is linear and tetas. And 2 is also linear in theta. Because again, it doesn't matter. That, for example, in this case, I have theta 2. And then I'm multiplying theta 2 with x2 and x3. That doesn't matter. What is happening, basically, is that when you're looking at your featured 2, so this is the bias term, this is feature 1, and this is feature 2, it just happens that you did feature in generating, that you're going to see, I think, next lecture, two lectures from now. text:
Audio: But let's see how we can use it in practice. So this L2 norm can be used in order to, in order to, again, measure the lengths or size of a vector. And it's also kind of a generalization of Pythagorean theorem. So in Pythagorean term, you had that, okay, if one side is A, one side is B, this other side is going to have the lengths of a school root of A squared plus B squared. But we also know is that we can use and generalize this notation and say that, okay, if you have a vector of A and B, which means that across the x dimension, you have A units of distance across the y dimension, you have B units of distance. The length of this vector from this equation is exactly going to be a score, which have A squared plus B squared. So kind of, it will tell you that this notion is going to help you measure the length of a vector. In addition to measuring the lengths of a vector, it can also help you measure the distance between two vectors. So for example, again, if I have two vectors here A and B, in order for me to measure the distance between A and B, effectively I can say that this vector right here is going to be the vector of A minus B. Right? That's the distance or the delta between A and B. And in order for me to measure the length of that A-B vector, I can calculate L2 norm of that vector A-B, which is what I wrote exactly right here. Video: 1: Lec 12 - DS100 Fa24 - Ordinary

audio: But let's see how we can use it in practice. So this L2 norm can be used in order to, in order to, again, measure the lengths or size of a vector. And it's also kind of a generalization of Pythagorean theorem. So in Pythagorean term, you had that, okay, if one side is A, one side is B, this other side is going to have the lengths of a school root of A squared plus B squared. But we also know is that we can use and generalize this notation and say that, okay, if you have a vector of A and B, which means that across the x dimension, you have A units of distance across the y dimension, you have B units of distance. The length of this vector from this equation is exactly going to be a score, which have A squared plus B squared. So kind of, it will tell you that this notion is going to help you measure the length of a vector. In addition to measuring the lengths of a vector, it can also help you measure the distance between two vectors. So for example, again, if I have two vectors here A and B, in order for me to measure the distance between A and B, effectively I can say that this vector right here is going to be the vector of A minus B. Right? That's the distance or the delta between A and B. And in order for me to measure the length of that A-B vector, I can calculate L2 norm of that vector A-B, which is what I wrote exactly right here.

Again, I'm not going to do vector calculus, but instead we're going to go with just intuition and geometric derivation of what's happening in this end-dimensional space that we're dealing with because we're in the end-dimensional space. What we have is that we have our Y vector as an end-dimensional vector. It has prediction for it has ground-fished values for all of the data points that we have or all of the observations and for each one of the observations we also have predicted values. So we have an end-dimensional vector and another end-dimensional vector that we're trying to make sure either as close to each other as possible. So we are in end-dimensional space, in a sense. And as you can imagine, as the size of the data set grows and as the number of features also grows, then the complexity of this model is going to grow and we're going to be in this higher dimensional space, which is hard to directly understand what's happening in it. So the last kind of reminder from linear algebra is a span. When I say I want to calculate a span of two, two vectors. So you can assume that these are my two vectors. Graph feature two, multiply all of those values with the parameter two and then so on and so forth. I kind of oversimplified it here. There's no teta zero, there's no other column. This is just a simplification of this case, assuming if you only have two columns. And this way, if you're thinking about getting all the predictions using a way that combination of columns, in a sense, your y hat is going to be a vector that's going to be in the span of your entire matrix X. Again, it's in the span of matrix X because of the way that I can think about how the predictions are being calculated. The predictions are being calculated by these specific weights that I have that are applied to the column vectors. And that's the definition of a span of X. So exactly what's happening, when I'm doing the prediction, is that I'm living in this end-dimensional space. Audio: Say, for example, one of the lights here is going to be the target valley or the target vector that I want to be in. Right? So effectively, what I want to do is I want to find a weighted combination of all of these features that is as close as possible to this target vector. But I can basically make up for this vertical distance. text:
Audio: Y hat is the orthogonal projection of Y unto the span of X. So we want that kind of degree to be kind of 90 degree. Meaning that we want to make sure that if you look at the distance between Y and also this Y hat that we have right here, this distance is minimized. And it is minimized at the place that this delta vector Y minus Y hat is minimized or is orthogonal to the entire span of X. So this is kind of that... Most of the take-aways that you want to take from the lecture today is going to be on this graph. Effectively, it means that the objective function that we have been trying to minimize is that we have been trying to minimize the lengths of this residual vector or y- minus y-hat. There is y, y is right here. There is y-hat, y-hat is right here, or I write it as x times theta. And in order to minimize this distance, I want to make sure that the length of this vector is minimized. And how can I make sure that the length of this vector is minimized? This vector should be the orthogonal projection of y unto this subspace that I'm living in. So we're going to formally define what this orthogonal projection means. And orthogonal projection, when you have two vectors in 2D actually means that they're perpendicular, meaning that the dot product between them is equal to 0. Remember that I was telling you that when you calculate the dot product, this is as if you're calculating the norm of A, and times the norm of B or the lengths of B, times the cosine of the angle between them. Audio: So this is going to be teta hat equal to x transpose times x inverse times x transpose times y. And this is the final solution for how to arrive at the optimum set of Heta values in the Teta vector having had the design matrix X and then having had your target values Y vector. And this is a close form solution for it. And this close-from-solution exists if and only if x transpose times x is invertible. And we're going to talk about... What is the condition that X transpose times X is going to be invertible? If it's not invertible, then in that case we can't actually have this term and we can directly solve it. It means that there is not going to be any unique solution, but there will be a solution. So, this is kind of a clean version of this. This is the final kind of last piece of this linear regression or multiple linear regression that we wanted to see that deserves one final slide or one separate slide by itself. So, kind of a summary of everything to be seen so far and kind of evaluating the performance of these models. There is a demo that we have that actually looks at this NDA data set. Go through all of this entire cycle. It looks that, okay, we're creating the data frame. We're creating. Audio: And then at the end, it will go ahead and do the evaluation. So in terms of the evaluation, previously, we had seen that we would do evaluation using the residual vector. So remember that the residual vector in simple linear regression should not have shown any kind of pattern. But if we go ahead and want to visualize the residual vector in our case, we won't have a specific feature to use in the x-axis, because we have multiple different features. Instead, the residual vector version that we're showing with multiple linear regression is always going to be showing the residual with respect to the Y hat values instead of with respect to each one of the individual features that we have. Still, we don't want to see any pattern. If we see any pattern, meaning that, okay, for example, in this case, it will perform better for Y hat, so there are smaller. So the values, the residuals are close to zero, but as the value of Y grows, then I'll love these, there's over predictions and under predictions that are happening. This is called Petra Sidescity, meaning that the performance of the model is not uniform across the Y axis or the Y-hat axis that we have. Again, we don't want to see a pattern in this case, regardless. We also calculate that root-minus-squared error in order to evaluate models last time with simple linear regression and constant model. text:
Audio: In the multiple linear regression, what we'll use is coefficient of determination, which is a fraction that we're calculating that talks about what is the variance of the fitted values divided by the variance of the ground-trose values. And S is what it does is it will tell you that what percent, what proportion of the variance in the underlying data are you capturing. So we want to make sure that we capture as much as possible of the underlying variance in the data so that we modeled everything and all the pattern that happened in the data. We can actually go ahead and if you take a look at the demo of notebook what you will see is that if I go ahead and just have a model that is a simple linear regression that just does the prediction based on the ASD feature the coefficient of determination is going to be 0.45 and as you keep adding features for example here I'm adding features of ASD and I'm using features of ASD and TRIP8 then the coefficient of determination is going to get To go higher in fact if you try and all of those three features together the coefficient of determination is going to be very close to one meaning that you explain the entire variance that you had in the entire data set that you have had. So there are some properties of this OLS or this model that we derived today. You're going to be simple. Some of them you're going to prove in the homework. But effectively, all of them are going to be derived from the fact that the residual vector is orthogonal to the span of X. Video: 1: v

Audio: So you're going to go ahead and basically see that this is the same property that we had that x transpose times residual should be equal to zero. And from that point, you will also be able to prove that the prediction vector transpose times residual vector should also be equal to zero because the prediction vector is also in this span of x. You will also prove in the homework that the summation of the residuals is equal to zero Because the residual was orthogonal to each one of the columns, it is also orthogonal to this column of all the values of 1s. And if you actually write it, this is going to basically give you that the summation of the residual values is equal to 1. I'm going to leave you with this fact that because the residual, the summation of the residuals is equal to 0, when there is a bias term, that's why we didn't use the residuals directly as an objective function. And we always calculated absolute value and also scores of the residuals. So we're going to stop here. There are more properties and the summation of every kind of model that you'd seen in all of the linear models. The last piece is that with the ordinary least square, the equation is always going to be by had equal to x times teta vector that derived actually how we can calculate the teta hat. And then we also derive this fact that there is a unique solution if and only if x transpose times x is invertible. Video: 1: Lec 12 - DS100 Fa24 - Ordinary
